license.notice: Event descriptions are paraphrased summaries written for this dataset. Source links point to authoritative references.

entries.title: Unsigned kernel module loaded
entries.summary: An unsigned or untrusted kernel module was detected loading.
entries.details: Generated when the Windows Code Integrity component detects that a kernel module (driver) that does not meet signing requirements has been loaded. Key fields: FileName (the module path) and ProcessNameBuffer (the process attempting the load). This event does not necessarily mean the load was blocked — it indicates detection. This is the primary detection signal for Bring Your Own Vulnerable Driver (BYOVD) attacks (T1068), where adversaries load a signed but vulnerable driver to exploit it for privilege escalation or to disable security tools. The FileName path is the highest-value pivot — evaluate against known vulnerable driver lists (e.g., LOLDrivers).

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools

investigationPivots: Hash the FileName and check against LOLDrivers (loldrivers.io) and VirusTotal
Review the driver's digital signature — a signed but known-vulnerable driver is a BYOVD indicator
Correlate with Sysmon EID 6 (driver load) for additional context including hash and signature details
Check for NtLoadDriver or ZwLoadDriver API calls in process activity near the event timestamp
commonFalsePositives: Legacy third-party drivers that predate mandatory kernel signing requirements (test-signed or older drivers)
Kernel debugging environments with test signing mode enabled

entries.title: File integrity check failed
entries.summary: Code Integrity could not verify the integrity of a file.
entries.details: Generated when Code Integrity is unable to verify the integrity of a file (e.g., due to a missing or inaccessible catalog, corrupted signature, or hash mismatch). Key fields: FileName and the error code. This event differs from EID 3033 (policy block) in that it indicates a verification failure rather than a policy decision. File integrity check failures on system binaries or security tools may indicate tampering, binary replacement, or in-memory patching. Repeated failures on a specific file path warrant inspection of the file's hash and signature.

investigationPivots: Hash FileName and compare against known-good file hashes from the vendor or Microsoft
Check whether the catalog (.cat) file referenced in the error is present and unmodified
Investigate whether the failure is isolated to one file or systemic across multiple binaries
commonFalsePositives: Incomplete software installation where catalog files have not yet been updated
Corrupted Windows Update leaving some files with mismatched catalog entries

entries.title: File hash not found in trusted catalog
entries.summary: The hash of a file was not found in any trusted catalog.
entries.details: Generated when Code Integrity cannot find the hash of a file in any trusted catalog file (.cat), meaning the file cannot be verified as genuine. Key fields: FileName. Unlike a signature failure, this indicates the file is not catalogued at all — it may be a novel file (malware), a renamed legitimate binary, or a file from a software package whose catalog has not been installed. This event is commonly seen with user-mode binaries in WDAC enforcement environments. For kernel-mode drivers, this is a strong indicator the driver is not part of any trusted package.

investigationPivots: Check FileName path — files in user-writable directories (AppData, Temp, Downloads) are high priority
Submit the file hash to VirusTotal or an internal threat intelligence platform
Correlate with process creation events to determine what process loaded the uncatalogued file
commonFalsePositives: Internal or custom software built in-house without catalog signing
Developer tools and test binaries not included in any published catalog

entries.title: Code Integrity blocked unsigned file (enforcement)
entries.summary: Code Integrity blocked a file from loading because it did not meet signing level requirements.
entries.details: Generated when Code Integrity actively blocks a file from loading because it does not meet the required signing level. Key fields: FileName, ProcessNameBuffer, and RequestedPolicy. This is the enforcement-mode counterpart to EID 3034 (audit). This event fires for both kernel-mode (driver) and user-mode (DLL/EXE) blocks depending on the active policy. For BYOVD investigation, a block on a kernel driver is the critical signal — it indicates the attacker attempted to load a driver and was stopped by Code Integrity or WDAC. Correlate with EID 3001 to determine whether an earlier detection event preceded the block.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Subvert Trust Controls: Code Signing Policy Modification

investigationPivots: FileName is the primary pivot — check against LOLDrivers and hash against threat intel
ProcessNameBuffer identifies the process attempting the load — unexpected loaders (non-system processes) are suspicious
If this event repeats for the same file, the attacker may be retrying after each failure — watch for persistence mechanisms
commonFalsePositives: Third-party software deploying drivers that have not been signed with the appropriate kernel signing certificate
Development or test environments where drivers are built locally without valid signatures

entries.title: Code Integrity audit: file would have been blocked
entries.summary: In audit mode, Code Integrity detected a file that would have been blocked under enforcement.
entries.details: Generated in audit mode (WDAC policy set to Audit Only) when Code Integrity detects a file that would have been blocked if enforcement were active. Key fields: FileName and ProcessNameBuffer. This event is used during WDAC policy deployment to identify what would break before switching to enforcement mode. In an incident response context, the presence of EID 3034 entries for suspicious files reveals that Code Integrity was in audit mode when an attacker loaded unsigned code — the load was not blocked. This event is critical for understanding WDAC posture gaps.

prerequisites.description: A Windows Defender Application Control (WDAC) policy must be deployed and active in audit mode for this event to be generated.

investigationPivots: Treat this event identically to EID 3033 from a threat hunting perspective — the load was permitted
Identify whether this host is intentionally in audit mode or if audit mode represents a misconfiguration
Use FileName to determine whether the loaded file was malicious
commonFalsePositives: Any unsigned file on a system in WDAC audit mode — volume is expected during policy baseline development
Internally developed software not yet added to the WDAC allow list

entries.title: WHQL policy violation
entries.summary: A file failed the Windows Hardware Quality Labs signing policy check.
entries.details: Generated when a kernel-mode driver fails the WHQL (Windows Hardware Quality Labs) signing enforcement check. WHQL signing is required for kernel drivers on Windows and indicates the driver has passed Microsoft hardware certification. A WHQL policy violation means the driver either lacks WHQL certification, has an expired signature, or uses a signing certificate not trusted for kernel code. This event is directly relevant to BYOVD attacks — adversaries frequently use drivers with expired or revoked WHQL certificates. Key field: FileName. Correlate with EID 3001 and EID 3033 for the same file path.

investigationPivots: Check the driver's signing certificate validity date — expired WHQL certs on known-vulnerable drivers is a BYOVD pattern
Cross-reference FileName hash against LOLDrivers (loldrivers.io) for known vulnerable driver matches
Identify the loading process — drivers loaded by non-system processes or from unusual paths are high priority
commonFalsePositives: Older hardware drivers with expired WHQL certificates that are otherwise benign
Hardware manufacturer drivers not submitted for WHQL certification (common with less mainstream devices)

entries.title: WDAC policy audit event
entries.summary: Windows Defender Application Control detected a file that would be blocked by the active policy (audit mode).
entries.details: Generated when a Windows Defender Application Control (WDAC) policy in audit mode detects a file that would be blocked if enforcement were active. Key fields: FileName, ProcessNameBuffer, PolicyName, and PolicyId. EID 3076 is the WDAC-specific audit event, whereas EID 3034 is the general Code Integrity audit event. In environments with named WDAC policies, PolicyName helps attribute the detection to the relevant rule set. This event is commonly used for WDAC policy tuning but in IR contexts reveals unsigned or untrusted code that executed while WDAC was not enforcing.

prerequisites.description: A Windows Defender Application Control (WDAC) policy must be deployed and active in audit mode for this event to be generated.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Subvert Trust Controls: Code Signing Policy Modification

investigationPivots: PolicyName identifies which WDAC rule triggered — useful for understanding what rule the attacker's file would violate
Correlate with EID 3089 to get additional context on the same file load event
In IR, treat audit events as successful loads — pivot on FileName to assess impact
commonFalsePositives: Unsigned internal software during WDAC policy baseline development
Scripting interpreters (python.exe, node.exe) executing user scripts not yet in the allow list

entries.title: WDAC policy enforcement block
entries.summary: Windows Defender Application Control blocked a file from executing under the active enforcement policy.
entries.details: Generated when a Windows Defender Application Control (WDAC) policy in enforcement mode blocks a file from loading or executing. Key fields: FileName, ProcessNameBuffer, PolicyName, and PolicyId. This is the enforcement-mode counterpart to EID 3076. EID 3077 indicates the file was actually blocked — unlike audit mode, the load did not succeed. In an attacker context, repeated EID 3077 events for the same file indicate the attacker is hitting WDAC policy repeatedly — they may subsequently attempt T1553.006 (Code Signing Policy Modification) to disable or bypass the active policy. Correlate with EID 3089 for full event correlation.

prerequisites.description: A Windows Defender Application Control (WDAC) policy must be deployed and active in enforcement mode for this event to be generated.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Subvert Trust Controls: Code Signing Policy Modification

investigationPivots: FileName is the primary artifact — hash and check against threat intel and LOLDrivers
ProcessNameBuffer identifies what attempted to load the blocked file — unexpected loaders are high priority
Multiple EID 3077 events for different files from the same process suggest a loader actively probing policy limits
commonFalsePositives: Software deployed without being added to the WDAC allow policy — common during initial rollout
Portable applications or scripts that are not signed and not yet catalogued

entries.title: WDAC correlated event
entries.summary: A correlated WDAC event providing supplemental context for a policy audit or enforcement event.
entries.details: Generated alongside EID 3076 (audit) or EID 3077 (enforcement) events to provide supplemental information about the WDAC policy decision. Key fields: FileName, PolicyName, PolicyId, and additional signing or hash details not included in the primary event. EID 3089 is typically generated in a 1:1 ratio with EID 3076/3077 and provides additional forensic context for the same file load. When investigating WDAC events, always review the correlated EID 3089 event for complete information about the file, signing state, and applicable policy.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Subvert Trust Controls: Code Signing Policy Modification

investigationPivots: Always pair EID 3089 with the corresponding EID 3076 or 3077 using the same FileName and timestamp
The signing details in EID 3089 provide the certificate information needed to assess driver trust
PolicyId links this event to the specific WDAC policy that generated the primary block or audit event
commonFalsePositives: Generated as a side-effect of any EID 3076/3077 event — treat as supplemental data, not an independent alert

entries.title: Code Integrity policy activated or deactivated
entries.summary: A Code Integrity or WDAC policy was loaded, refreshed, or deactivated.
entries.details: Generated when a Code Integrity or Windows Defender Application Control (WDAC) policy is activated, refreshed, or deactivated on the system. Key fields: PolicyNameBuffer, PolicyId, and whether the policy is being enabled or disabled. Adversaries may attempt to disable or replace WDAC policies as part of T1553.006 (Code Signing Policy Modification) to allow unsigned or malicious code to run. A policy deactivation event — especially if followed immediately by EID 3034/3076 audit events that previously would have been enforced blocks — is a high-confidence indicator of intentional defense impairment. This event should also fire after legitimate policy deployments.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Subvert Trust Controls: Code Signing Policy Modification

investigationPivots: Verify whether the policy change was authorized — correlate with change management records or admin activity
A policy deactivation followed by new EID 3034 or EID 3001 events indicates enforcement was intentionally bypassed
PolicyId identifies which policy was changed — correlate with the expected active policy set for this host
commonFalsePositives: Authorized IT/security team deploying an updated WDAC policy baseline
Group Policy refresh applying a new WDAC policy to managed endpoints
